mission.title: Install and Configure Kubeflow on Kubernetes
mission.description: Kubeflow is a powerful Machine Learning Toolkit for Kubernetes that provides a modular and scalable platform for building AI applications. Installing Kubeflow allows you to leverage a comprehensive set of tools for the entire AI lifecycle, from model training to serving.

steps.title: Add the Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the Kubeflow Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.
```bash
helm repo add kubeflow https://alauda.github.io/kubeflow-chart
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Kubeflow using Helm
steps.description:
Install Kubeflow in a dedicated namespace. This command will create the namespace if it doesn't exist.
```bash
helm install kubeflow kubeflow/kubeflow --namespace kubeflow --create-namespace --version 1.6.2
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that all pods are running in the Kubeflow namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kubeflow
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Set resource limits for the Kubeflow components by applying a custom configuration. Modify the `kubeflow-config.yaml` as needed and apply it.
```bash
kubectl apply -f kubeflow-config.yaml --namespace kubeflow
```

steps.title: Access the Kubeflow dashboard
steps.description:
Port-forward the Kubeflow dashboard service to access it locally.
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/istio-ingressgateway -n kubeflow 8080:80
```

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Kubeflow release from your cluster.
```bash
helm uninstall kubeflow --namespace kubeflow
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and resources
uninstall.description:
Delete any remaining Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and other resources associated with Kubeflow.
```bash
kubectl delete crd $(kubectl get crd | grep kubeflow | awk '{print $1}')
```

uninstall.title: Remove the namespace
uninstall.description:
Delete the Kubeflow namespace to clean up any remaining resources.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace kubeflow
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up your current Kubeflow configuration and resources.
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace kubeflow -o yaml > kubeflow-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update the Helm repository
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository to fetch the latest charts.
```bash
helm repo update
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade Kubeflow
upgrade.description:
Upgrade your existing Kubeflow installation to the latest version.
```bash
helm upgrade kubeflow kubeflow/kubeflow --namespace kubeflow --version 1.6.2
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that all pods are running after the upgrade.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kubeflow
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in Pending state
troubleshooting.description:
If you see pods in a Pending state, check for resource availability in your cluster.
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> --namespace kubeflow
```

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If the Kubeflow dashboard is not accessible, ensure that the port-forward command is running and check the service status.
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace kubeflow
```

troubleshooting.title: Helm release failed
troubleshooting.description:
If the Helm installation fails, check the Helm release status for errors.
```bash
helm status kubeflow --namespace kubeflow
```

troubleshooting.title: Configuration changes not applied
troubleshooting.description:
If changes to configurations are not reflected, ensure you applied the correct YAML file and check for errors in the logs.
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace kubeflow
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Kubeflow will have all components running in the 'kubeflow' namespace, and you will be able to access the Kubeflow dashboard via the specified port. Ensure that all pods are in the Running state.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kubeflow https://alauda.github.io/kubeflow-chart
helm repo update

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and have installed Helm and kubectl on your local machine.
